en.extract:
The South Georgia diving petrel or Georgian diving-petrel (Pelecanoides georgicus) is one of five very similar small auk-like diving petrels of the southern oceans. It is native to the South Atlantic and islands of the southern Indian Ocean and south-eastern Australia.


Taxonomy and nomenclature
The American ornithologist Robert Cushman Murphy and the zoologist Francis Harper described the South Georgia diving petrel in 1916. Its specific name, georgicus, is derived from the South Georgia islands where they identified the species. Other common names include puffinure de Géorgie du Sud (French), Breitschnabel Lummensturmvogel (German), and potoyunco de Georgia (Spanish). A unique New Zealand population is now considered a separate species, the Whenua Hou diving petrel.


Description
The South Georgia diving petrel is a small, plump petrel, 180 to 220 mm (7.1–8.7 in) in length and weighing around 90 to 150 g (3.2–5.3 oz). Its plumage is black above and dull white below, and it has a stubby black bill with pale blue edges. The wings have thin white strips. The face and neck can be more brown than black. The legs are blue with posterior black lines down the tarsi. Unless seen very close, it is almost indistinguishable from the common diving petrel; the common diving petrel has brown inner web primary feathers, whereas the South Georgia diving petrel has light inner web feathering. Common diving petrels have smaller and narrower bills than the South Georgia diving petrel, and there are also slight size differences.


Distribution and habitat
This species nests in colonies on Subantarctic islands. It breeds on South Georgia in the south Atlantic and on the Prince Edward Islands, Crozet Islands, Kerguelen Islands and Heard Island and McDonald Islands in the southern Indian Ocean. It disperses to surrounding seas and vagrants have been recorded in the Falkland Islands and Australia. While 1.5 m (4.9 ft) nesting burrows are usually built on scree slopes above the vegetation line, they are occasionally built on flat land. 


Behaviour

The South Georgia diving petrel feeds primarily on planktonic crustaceans, particularly krill, but will also feed on small fish and young cephalopods. Breeding season is October–February. The female lays one egg that is incubated for 44–52 days. Fledging occurs in 43–60 days. Threats to the South Georgia diving petrel include skuas, cats and rats. South Georgia diving petrels are noted for their diving capabilities: "The most proficient divers of the order Procellariformes are likely to be the diving petrels in the family Pelecanoididae." Dive depths for the South Georgia diving petrel have been recorded to 48.6 m (159 ft), with most in the range of 20.4 to 24.4 m (67–80 ft).


References

The '''South Georgia diving petrel''' or '''Georgian diving-petrel''' ('''''Pelecanoides georgicus''''') is one of five very similar small [[auk]]-like [[diving petrel]]s of the southern oceans. It is native to the [[Atlantic Ocean|South Atlantic]] and islands of the southern [[Indian Ocean]] and south-eastern [[Australia]].

==Taxonomy and nomenclature==
The American [[ornithology|ornithologist]] [[Robert Cushman Murphy]] and the zoologist [[Francis Harper (biologist)|Francis Harper]] described the South Georgia diving petrel in 1916.{{r|IUCN}}<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Murphy | first1=Robert Cushman | author1-link=Robert Cushman Murphy | last2=Harper | first2=Francis|  author2-link=Francis Harper (biologist) | year=1916 | title=Two new diving petrels | journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History | volume=35 | pages=65–67 | url=http://digitallibrary.amnh.org/bitstream/handle/2246/1843//v2/dspace/ingest/pdfSource/bul/B035a07.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y }}</ref> Its specific name, ''georgicus,'' is derived from the [[South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands#South Georgia|South Georgia]] islands where they identified the species. Other common names include ''puffinure de Géorgie du Sud'' (French), ''Breitschnabel Lummensturmvogel'' (German), and ''potoyunco de Georgia'' (Spanish).{{r|ibc}} A unique New Zealand population is now considered a separate species, the [[Whenua Hou diving petrel]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Fischer|first1=Johannes H.|last2=Debski|first2=Igor|last3=Miskelly|first3=Colin M.|last4=Bost|first4=Charles A.|last5=Fromant|first5=Aymeric|last6=Tennyson|first6=Alan J. D.|last7=Tessler|first7=Jake|last8=Cole|first8=Rosalind|last9=Hiscock|first9=Johanna H.|date=2018-06-27|title=Analyses of phenotypic differentiations among South Georgian Diving Petrel (''Pelecanoides georgicus'') populations reveal an undescribed and highly endangered species from New Zealand|journal=PLOS ONE|language=en|volume=13|issue=6|article-number=e0197766|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0197766|pmid=29949581|pmc=6021066|bibcode=2018PLoSO..1397766F|issn=1932-6203|doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Description==
The South Georgia diving petrel is a small, plump petrel, {{convert|180|to(-)|220|mm|in|abbr=on}} in length and weighing around {{convert|90|to(-)|150|g|oz|abbr=on}}.{{r|polar}} Its [[plumage]] is black above and dull white below, and it has a stubby black bill with pale blue edges.{{r|adw}} The wings have thin white strips. The face and neck can be more brown than black. The legs are blue with posterior black lines down the [[Tarsus (eyelids)|tarsi]].{{r|adw}} Unless seen very close, it is almost indistinguishable from the [[common diving petrel]]; the common diving petrel has brown inner web primary feathers, whereas the South Georgia diving petrel has light inner web feathering. Common diving petrels have smaller and narrower bills than the South Georgia diving petrel,{{r|adw}}{{r|Brooke}} and there are also slight size differences.{{r|diff}}

==Distribution and habitat==
This species nests in colonies on [[Subantarctic]] islands. It breeds on South Georgia in the south Atlantic and on the [[Prince Edward Islands]], [[Crozet Islands]], [[Kerguelen Islands]] and [[Heard Island and McDonald Islands]] in the southern Indian Ocean.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Holdaway|first1=Richard N.|last2=Jones|first2=Martin D.|last3=Beavan Athfield|first3=Nancy R.|date=2003|title=Establishment and extinction of a population of South Georgian diving petrel (''Pelecanoides georgicus'') at Mason Bay, Stewart Island, New Zealand, during the late Holocene|journal=Journal of the Royal Society of New Zealand|volume=33|issue=3|pages=601–622|doi=10.1080/03014223.2003.9517748|s2cid=140188250|doi-access=free|bibcode=2003JRSNZ..33..601H }}</ref><ref name="Enderby">{{Cite journal|last1=Fischer|first1=Johannes H.|last2=Hjorsvarsdottir|first2=Freydis O.|last3=Hiscock|first3=Johanna A.|last4=Debski|first4=Igor|last5=Taylor|first5=Graeme A.|last6=Wittmer|first6=Heiko U.|date=2017|title=Confirmation of the extinction of South Georgian diving petrels (''Pelecanoides georgicus'') on Enderby Island|url=https://notornis.osnz.org.nz/system/files/Fischer%20et%20al%202017%20Notornis%2064%2048_51.pdf|journal=Notornis|volume=64|pages=48–51|doi=10.63172/039024mccokx |access-date=14 May 2018|archive-date=14 May 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180514142048/https://notornis.osnz.org.nz/system/files/Fischer%20et%20al%202017%20Notornis%2064%2048_51.pdf}}</ref> It disperses to surrounding seas and [[vagrancy in birds|vagrants]] have been recorded in the [[Falkland Islands]] and [[Australia]].{{r|IUCN}} While {{convert|1.5|m|ft|abbr=on}} nesting burrows are usually built on scree slopes above the vegetation line, they are occasionally built on flat land.{{r|polar}}{{r|adw}} 

==Behaviour==
[[File:Puffinure de Géorgie MHNT.jpg|thumb|Egg of ''Pelecanoides georgicus '']]
The South Georgia diving petrel feeds primarily on [[plankton]]ic [[crustacean]]s, particularly [[krill]], but will also feed on small fish and young [[cephalopod]]s. Breeding season is October–February.{{r|BirdLife}} The female lays one egg that is incubated for 44–52 days. Fledging occurs in 43–60 days.{{r|polar}} Threats to the South Georgia diving petrel include [[skua]]s, cats and rats. South Georgia diving petrels are noted for their diving capabilities: "The most proficient divers of the order ''Procellariformes'' are likely to be the diving petrels in the family ''Pelecanoididae''."{{r|dive}} Dive depths for the South Georgia diving petrel have been recorded to {{convert|48.6|m|ft|abbr=on}}, with most in the range of {{convert|20.4|to(-)|24.4|m|ft|abbr=on}}.{{r|dive}}
